sentences.text: Well, Madame Rothschild was a saver of children.
But she was able to do this even with the knowledge of the Gestapo.
She was like a warehouse for children almost, because they would come all the time to her house, to that big building, and requesting as many children that they needed to fill their 1,000 quota.
They also came one day to ask for the Wagschal children, absolutely asking for the three of us.
The woman, Madame Rothschild, had the courage to plead for us not to be taken away, because their mother is in the hospital.
The courage is unbelievable, to speak back to a Gestapo.
Miracle.
They allowed us to stay.
Unfortunately, for one that doesn't go, another one goes, because they needed to have quotas.
So sometimes they came and picked up children by name.
And sometimes they picked up children just by quota numbers, to make their 1,000 quota.
Yes.
Yes.
And I believe that is why Madame Rothschild separated Jackie and Sammy from her house into other hiding places.
And she held one child back for herself.
And I wanted to bring out another thing that happened there.
During my stay there-- I cannot indicate how long we stayed in that place-- but I can say without-- maybe about a year or eight months.
But I cannot indicate the time.
There used to be a boy of-- at that time he was 11 years old, going on 12.
He was the only non-Jewish boy there.
And by the way, Madame Rothschild is a Jew.
This boy did something that was unfortunate.
I used to have a problem.
I used to wet my pants all the time.
That's comes from fear.
I did not have the facility to always clean myself.
So I developed red rashes around my private parts.
And it would, it would burn terribly.
And one day this boy said, you know, I think I can help you.
So he went and he took me to the bathroom.
He set himself on the toilet, put me on his lap with my legs spread on either side of his thighs.
He told me to take my two hands and put them over my eyes and not to peep.
And thus, he began to molest me.
This went on for several times.
And one day I peeped through my fingers and realized what he was doing was wrong.
I told him never again to touch me.
And I did not-- I was not devastated from it.
I was not traumatized from it, because he was gentle.
But I could analyze it as something, again, I had no control over.
It was just part of a situation that arose because of the environment that was created.
And that a boy of 12 had to go somehow and picked on a person who had a problem, and saw it as something he could take advantage to to help himself.
No, this was a secret.
I kept that secret for-- I, I told it to my mother many, many, many, many years later in Brooklyn, New York.
And she was in shock.
But she's OK.
She was OK about it, because I'm cool about it.
The thing is that I could not accept the fact on one side, and yet I was not traumatized, because I-- I was always analytical.
So I forgave the boy.
But I told him never to touch me again.
But something very unfortunate happened to this boy after we moved from that place to another place.
But life in that place was one big nightmare.

It was hell on Earth, because when you hear the sirens and the bombs, and you don't know where those bombs are going to fall, and you're standing there shaking like a leaf, you don't know if you're going to live or die.
And this happens not once a week, or twice a week, but almost every day.
So when you are and that kind of mind, you think about one thing all day long.
How am I going to survive till tomorrow?
So just thinking was our workload for the day.
How we were able to survive.
But unfortunately, we could not stay any longer there.
Madame Rothschild had to run, because now they were looking for her.
So the whole group of children and Madame Rothschild--
I could not verify by numbers, but I could say easily between 60 and 80 children.
We went to a place known as Land, L-A-N-D.
And over there there were lots of orchards and gardens in the environment.
It was more in the country.
And I remember one night, I was sleeping in a room with three double bunk beds.
And there was a closet, a makeshift closet.
It had no doors, but it had a cloth hanging so that you can have privacy, because behind that was a chamber pot.
And one night I learned why they came, the Gestapo came.
And a little girl was sitting behind that curtain on the chamber pot in the room I was sleeping in.
And as I am looking out, from my eyes, I see the Gestapo taking this big boot and just hitting on this child.
And the next day I learned that they were looking for children 12 and over.
See, they had these quotas all the time.
I was seven.
And this, the only child that they took away from Landen, Belgium, that night was the only non-Jewish child, the same boy who had molested me.
What a coincidence.
I felt bad.
He didn't deserve that.
If he were alive today I would hug him, because I understand.
I-- I understand, and I understood that sometimes we do things out of need, out of wanting to be loved, and most of all, for survival.
It is also in Landen where one night the bombs were dropping very heavy, and right next to us was a big house too.
That burned.
And all around the building where us Jewish children we're staying, all around, there was fire.
It's almost like a burning inferno, and we stood there like a bush that never got touched.
If God doesn't play miracles, he played there a miracle.
Believe me.
That was a miracle.
And we survived Landen.
In Landen I got horrific rashes, all again from tension and nerves.
They had to tie my hands and sweaters, so I would not scratch myself to bleeding.
I had whooping cough there.
I was always sick during the war from the fear-- that fear, that constant fear-- and lack of food-- starvings, eating very meager, meagerly.
You cannot have the proper nutrition during war.
It's impossible.
So you eat whatever is possible to get on the table.
And Madame Rothschild always tried to see that we had something decent in our stomach so we could survive.
But then we got note again.
We must run.
The Gestapos are again behind Madame Rothschild and her children.
So we all, in the middle of the night-- that was so traumatic-- we all clothed ourselves fast.
And I don't know how we got there, where it is, but we were placed in a handicapped institution in a small village.
For my eyes, having never been in front of handicapped people, and seeing an institution of this nature, was again very, very traumatic.
But I adapted, and we stayed there several months.

And one night, the villagers-- who were very much for the people who were suffering, not for Hitler and his war-- they took a dummy, a Hitler dummy, and they placed it in the middle of the square, and they did one night a bonfire with Hitler burning.
And I remember we were cheering that.
So we had our moments of laughter amidst all our tears and fears, because comedy is very important too.
A little humor never hurts.
It kindles the spirit.
This was, by far, a teacher right there, to be in that institution, because it taught me to appreciate life even more so because I had my eyes, my hands, my legs.
I had still my person.
Oh, yes.
I spent days with them.
I-- I-- I was sitting with them.
We all sat together.
We ate together.
They were the kindest, most wonderful, inspirational group of people I've ever met in my life, in Belgium.
I mean, I learned from the handicapped so much.
And you know what I learned most from them?
Humility.
They-- you know, I mean, I was only seven.
But they, they were so-- they showed such great courage of being who they were, and so dignified, that I got inspired to survive through them.
Sometimes we wanted to give up.
Why we keep running?
But I got inspired.
And I wanted to live more than ever, especially because Madame Rothschild was such a darling woman, and always so caring for all of us.
And so that experience led us to more hiding places.
And we ended up in a place known as Auderghem.
And Auderghem is where I stayed till the end of the war.
And it is over there that we had a little bit-- this was the year, the latter part of '44, the early part of '45.
And I know that because both Sam and Jack told me that we came together when it was very warm.
So we all presume that it was the summer time, sometimes in July, that we got together.
And I keep saying no, it had to be May.
But they keep saying it was so warm that we couldn't even put on a jacket.
So in Belgium when it's hot, it's 75 degrees.
[LAUGHS] It's not like Miami.

And in those days, in-- in the Belgian climate, you have a lot-- a lot of rain, which penetrates right through your bones, and-- and snow sometimes in certain parts, especially the [NON-ENGLISH] area, et cetera.
But you don't have very much of a dry, sunny day, except for sometimes in the two months July, and August.
In Auderghem I found more peace.
I became even more attached to Madame Rothschild.
And her husband, who was never in vision during all this time that we were hiding and running, finally came to Auderghem.
And she reunited with her husband.
And he was such a wonderful person.
And there I remember I would peel potatoes.
I will clean green beans.
That was a luxury, to clean potatoes, to clean green beans, because remember, before I always was eating turnips.
And it was there that I was given by Madame Rothschild, and several other children, a piece of land to grow vegetables.
And I grew poppy seed and the most jumbo-sized radishes, the-- the red variety.
And when my mother came-- see what I'm bringing in now, I'm just giving you quickly my own personal experiences, which are, you cannot really detail everything on video.
I just want to bring my mother and father experiences in, because they are not alive to bring out their experiences.
So I want to bring as much as my mother and father into this.
But when my mother came to pick me up in 1945 sometimes--

We all were reunited, because the-- the Jewish Underground, the Jewish Joint, all of these organization, and some Catholic Belgian women, they all made sure that all the children that were placed were always coming-- were going to come back as families.
Everybody was written about, the names and so forth.
So we-- they always knew where everybody was.
And when my mother came to pick me up, to take me home to Antwerp, I did not want to go with her.
I was frightened of her.
And the reason I was frightened, Sontha, is because in front of me stood a woman 68 pounds of weight. 68 pounds.
And that frightened me to see nothing but a skeleton in front.
But she coached me, and we came home.
Oh, I was always making beds, working in the kitchen, working on the land.
I always made sure that my day was productive.
I didn't like to laze around.
I had to be productive.
As a matter of fact, it was in Auderghem that I saw my first movie in my life.
It was That's how-- that was my first movie in my life, at age nine.
So that was quite an experience right there, fantasyland.
--and father--
--and my two brothers.
And my sister, Sara-Golde, she was born in 1950, after the war.
Yes.
We all lived at 32 Van Diepenbeeckstraat.
We stayed there until 1953.
And on June 3, 1953, with the help of the Jewish Joint, we migrated to the United States.
Would you believe the coincidence?
Zurenborgstraat before the war is, and still is a one-block street, located between Lange Leemstraat and Mercatorstraat.
And Van Diepenbeeckstraat is adjacent to Zurenborgstraat, which is also one street, one-block street, located between Lange Leem and Mercator.
We lived before the war on one side, on the left side, and by dividing by-- by a block, on the other side, which was Van Diepenbeeckstraat.
And the house we lived in, which was donated by the Belgian government, we paid the minimum of 500 Belgian francs a month in rent.
Many times we didn't have the rent, and the Jewish Joint helped us to pay the rent.
Many times when we did not have the funds to eat, my brothers and I would go to the Jewish Joint and eat a meal.
Many times if we couldn't afford certain clothes, the Jewish Joint would always clothe us, when my father didn't have the money to clothe us.
As much as he fed us and dressed us, there were times where he could not.
The Jewish Joint was always there to help.
The organization is unbelievable.
In the Jewish life, we were-- we are taught from the beginning always to do tzedakah.
And I think that is where lies our strength-- to help each other, to stand by each other, to survive together, into the philosophy we are raised in.
And when we divide too much, then the tree and its branches start to fall off.
So to always do for each other gives strength to the root of the tree.
When I came to Antwerp, I was nine years old.
And the following year, I remember very well, my parents gave me for my tenth birthday a roll of pink toilet paper-- that was a big deal-- and a blue plastic belt.
That was a big deal.
Now the house we lived in was a concierge house for a synagogue.
It had, on the street level, one door with stoops and a door that opened up with a ding-a-ling-ling bell.
That led to our hallway to go upstairs to our quarters.
Next to that was two very large doors.
When you opened them-- you-- people who used to go to the synagogue before the war would enter this.
And they would walk all the way down in the back to the synagogue.
The synagogue, unfortunately, was bombed during the war, so it was not in use after the war.
And my brother Jackie found the most gorgeous menorah there, which was re-donated to the actual owners.
And the two boys and myself, we used to play in that synagogue.
That was our playground, that synagogue.
It was very lovely, very sweet, very nice.
And right next to it was an asphalt place with chickens crawling.
And next to that were a couple houses.
And next to that was an army barracks with kazerne and so forth.
And every morning after the war I woke up with a bugle sound.
And opposite that was a limousine service.
All this in one block.
[LAUGHS] It was unbelievable.
And it was very intimate.
Life in Belgium was very intimate, small streets.
Everybody knows each other.
Very provincial.
And my father, who as you know, was very orthodox, he used to go to the shtibl, the shtibl of the [NON-ENGLISH] and the [NON-ENGLISH].
Both of these men are cousins to my father.
And I would sometimes go to the main synagogue, but not to pray.
I went there to look at boys, because everybody was decked in their finest, et cetera, et cetera.
And we would just flirt with each other and so forth But I went to the shtibl myself.
And I went to the Beit Yaakov school.
I studied Chumash and Nach, and knew all my prayers.
Some were very long, especially the one for the bathroom.
And learned from the fundamental-- learning from the fundamental of Judaism and its history, et cetera, et cetera, how we as a people were always chased, and chased, and chased, and chased.
And the chase has not stopped, because we are being chased today.
And I would like to say this, that each and every time, when a person in the United States says this Holocaust never happened, it is like a dagger that goes right through all of us survivors.
When we live day to day, we have put it in the subconscious so we can survive the conscious.
But when you hitting our subconscious, we immediately are back 50 years ago when it happened.
That means we are constantly sacrificing to relive our lives during the duress, and stress, and pain, and fear, and agonies, for survival that we went through in that war experience.
And we, who are now contributing to this wonderful foundation that Mr.
Steven Spielberg has put together-- I feel honored to be here.
Because as an American, for him to have had the sensitivity to create a foundation like this, I honor him for this.

And we who have contributed, by choice-- no one told me to do this-- by choice, is because we want to make sure, even though it hurts so deeply inside to relive the experience, that this tragedy, this absolute negativity of the human element, must never repeat itself.
I pray.
Sontha, I-- my schooling was very limited.
Very, very, very limited.
There was no time to educate oneself.
So I would say almost nothing.

I was just trying with my mind to find a way to-- how am I going to do today, to be able to go through when you're shaking like this, and you hear the bombs falling, and the, and the, and the, the explosions, and the fires, and the sirens.
Every time the siren, and you have to turn all the lights off.
And you have to run in the basement, and you have no clothes on, and you're shaking from cold, you're shaking from fear.
Education meant nothing, nothing, because you see the mind-- we didn't eat food.
We didn't eat brain food.
We had to use our own adrenaline to-- to survive with whatever we could.
So there was no ways to be able to sit and calmly study.
Because to study you have to be calm.
You have to be able to concentrate.
There was no concentration here, ability, because it was so hard.
Oh, yes, I read books.
Here and there we would look, we'd glance.
But it was always with one ear open, one eye open, because you didn't know where else you're going to be the next moment, because we were always in hiding.
This was a hiding element of three to four years for Jewish children all over Europe, my country being Belgium.
No.
Not at all.
I did not know nothing about my mother and father.
Only after I met them.
And then we-- and we got together.
And then I'm going to tell you now a little bit about the two of them.
Because they are dead, I must speak for them now to keep the Wagschal name and the honor of them also having been Holocaust survivors in this beautiful foundation and for the world.
